coredata.dc:title: SnO2/carbon dots nanocomposite synthesized by one-pot solvothermal technique for high-sensitivity triethylamine gas sensors
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Yuehong
Liu, Yukun
Wang, Youchao
Cheng, Yujie
Li, Yongchao
Cao, Guohua
Zong, Haitao
Li, Ming
Yan, Lingling
Zhang, Baoqing
coredata.dc:description: The exploration of triethylamine (TEA) gas sensor with low cost, high response and low detection limit is still an urgent challenge. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) activated spherical Tin oxide (SnO2) nanocomposites were synthesized by a simple and friendly one-pot hydrothermal strategy, and CDs with rich functional groups are in-situ introduced into SnO2. SnO2/CDs composites were characterized by X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, etc. The gas sensing properties of SnO2/CDs and SnO2 for triethylamine (TEA) were systematically investigated. The sensor based on SnO2/CDs exhibits a highly sensitive response, good selectivity, repeatability and long stability. It shows the response (R a/R g = 55) to 100 ppm TEA is approximately 1.8 times that of SnO2 sensor. More attractively, the detection limit of the SnO2/CDs composite to TEA is 90 ppb, which is 6.7 times that of SnO2. The significantly improved TEA sensing performance of SnO2/CDs sensing material is mainly attributed to the formation of heterojunction and the increased active sites due to the introduction of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SnO2/CDs
Heterojunction
Gas sensor
Triethylamine
Solvothermal synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and measurement of gas sensor
Structural and morphological characteristics of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs

Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research is supported by Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province, China (Grant No. 232300421203), the Key Science and Technology Program of Henan Province, China (Grant No. 202102310228) and Basic scientific research business cost project of Henan Polytechnic University, China (Grant no. NSFRF210328).

The research is supported by Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province , China (Grant No. 212300410348 ), the Key Science and Technology Program of Henan Province , China (Grant No. 202102310228 ) and Basic scientific research business cost project of Henan Polytechnic University , China (Grant no. NSFRF210328 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs. (b) Fluorescence spectra of SnO2, CDs and SnO2/CDs under.
SEM images and elemental mapping images of SnO2 (a,c) and SnO2/CDs (b,d).
TEM, HRTEM and corresponding SAED images of SnO2 (a,b, c) and SnO2/CDs (d, e, f).
FTIR spectra of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs.
Survey scans of XPS spectra of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs, (a,b) full spectra, (c,d) Sn3d profiles，(e,f) O1s profiles.

(a) Response of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs to 100 ppm TEA at 200–350 °C. (b) Dynamic response-recovery curve of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs to 10–1000 ppm TEA, (Inset) Response-recovery curve to 10–50 ppm TEA. (c) Corresponding fitted sensor response of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs, (Inset) Fitted sensor response to 10–100 ppm. (d) Responses of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs to 100 ppm various gases.

(a) Response and recovery curves of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs to 100 ppm TEA, (b) Cycles, (c) stability of response of SnO2/CDs to 100 ppm TEA, and (d) Relationship between sensing responses and relative humidity (RH%).
Schematic diagram of the sensing mechanism of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs for the response to TEA.
Weight ratio and elemental ratio of Sn,O and C in products of SnO2 and (d) SnO2/CDs.
Comparison of TEA gas sensors based on various SnO2 MOS gas sensors.

head.ce:title: SnO2/carbon dots nanocomposite synthesized by one-pot solvothermal technique for high-sensitivity triethylamine gas sensors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronic Information Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Electronic Information Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Electronic Information Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo 454000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo 454000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The exploration of triethylamine (TEA) gas sensor with low cost, high response and low detection limit is still an urgent challenge. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) activated spherical Tin oxide (SnO2) nanocomposites were synthesized by a simple and friendly one-pot hydrothermal strategy, and CDs with rich functional groups are in-situ introduced into SnO2. SnO2/CDs composites were characterized by X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, etc. The gas sensing properties of SnO2/CDs and SnO2 for triethylamine (TEA) were systematically investigated. The sensor based on SnO2/CDs exhibits a highly sensitive response, good selectivity, repeatability and long stability. It shows the response (R a/R g = 55) to 100 ppm TEA is approximately 1.8 times that of SnO2 sensor. More attractively, the detection limit of the SnO2/CDs composite to TEA is 90 ppb, which is 6.7 times that of SnO2. The significantly improved TEA sensing performance of SnO2/CDs sensing material is mainly attributed to the formation of heterojunction and the increased active sites due to the introduction of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Triethylamine (TEA) is a volatile organic compound (VOCs), commonly used as catalysts, energy fuels and preservative in chemical and food industries [1,2]. TEA possesses the strong fishy malodorous, and can serve as a reliable indicator for evaluating the freshness of fish and seafood. Moreover, owing to its explosive properties, TEA vapors are flammable at high temperatures or in open flames [3,4]. In environments with high concentrations of TEA (above 10 ppm indoors), it can cause significant irritation to the skin, eyes, respiratory system, and even die. So far, lots of methods for detecting TEA, such as electrochemiluminescence, gas chromatography and colorimetry, have been developed and achieved good detection results. However, the complex detection process, long running cycles, and high costs limit their wide application [5]. Therefore, researchers have been working to explore accurate TEA sensors with simple operation and low cost.

Gas sensors based on semiconductor metal oxide (MOS) have gained widespread attention due to their high sensitivity, low cost and real-time operation [6,7]. Numerous MOS materials (such as SnO2 [4], V2O5 [7], MoO3 [8], In2O3 [9], WO3 [10], ZnO [11]) are explored to detect TEA. Among them, rutile-type SnO2, a typical n-type semiconductor with a wide band gap of 3.6 eV, is one of the most important materials for semiconductor sensors due to high electron mobility and excellent physical and chemical stability [11]. However, the gas sensor based on single-phase SnO2 exhibits relative high detection limit and low response value for TEA, which cannot meet the practical requirement. In order to improve the performance of the sensor, various techniques have been employed, such as doping [12], morphological control and heterostructure construction [13,14]. Wei et al. investigated Au doping SnO2 mesoporous nanospheres, and present a high response (5.1 for 5 ppm TEA) and a low detection limit (110 ppb) due to increased active sites and easier oxygen adsorption [15]. By constructing heterojunctions with MOS materials and generating carrier accumulation layers at the interfaces, the charge transfer ability could be improved, thereby increasing the gas sensing response [10,16]. Meng reported SiO2/SnO2 PN hierarchical heterojunction with the improved response value of 18 for 100 ppm TEA [17]. Sun et al. prepared hierarchical porous SnO2/Zn2SnO4 composites through hydrothermal method combined post heat-treatment, and demonstrated the great contribution of the heterojunction between two materials to the sensing performance [18]. Sensitive materials with abundant active sites and high electron transport rate typically show promising gas adsorption and reactivity capabilities. Zhang et al. reported SnO2/ZrO2 heterojunction with a three-dimensionally ordered microstructure (3DOM) provided superior selectivity and a lower detection limit of 40 ppb [19], and pointed out that the atomic layer deposition (ALD) process ensures the orderly structure of material, but the preparation apparatus is complex and expensive. In contrast, the chemical hydrothermal method offers simple growth operations, a wide selection of raw materials, and easy introduction of heterogeneous materials with abundant active sites and high electronic conductivity.

Unlike inorganic metal oxides, carbon dots (CDs) are small-sized nanomaterials composed of carbon skeletons, which have some advantages of low cost, easy fabrication, remarkable conductivity and rich surface functional groups. In addition, CDs possess graphene-like lattice parameters and abundant active sites, and thus the heterojunction between CDs and MOS can be formed easily, uniformly and efficiently. In result, CDs is attributed to abundant functional groups presence, enhanced adsorbed oxygen, and electronic regulation to MOS, which is crucial for the sensing performance enhancement [20–22]. Yu et al. investigated the sensor based on ZnO/CDs hybrid prepared by blending, and presented the significantly improved sensitivity to NO (from 2.8 of pristine ZnO increasing to 238 of ZnO/CDs for 100 ppm NO) [23]. Lv et al. utilized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots to modify ultrathin mesoporous SnO2 nanosheets, and the corresponding sensors showed improved gas sensing response to NO2, and effectively reduced the operating temperature to 130 °C [24]. Zhang et al. reported that the carbonized polymer dots (CPDs)/WO3 heterostructure sensor exhibited extraordinary sensing characteristics for TEA detection. Strong coupling between CPDs and MOS realizes effective gas adsorption and efficient charge transfer [25], suggesting that CDs play a positive role in gas detection of nitrogen compounds. The above composites were fabricated using a two-step method, i.e., CDs were first prepared and then mixed with precursor solution of MOS. On the other hand, the composites prepared in a one-step method have gained attention due to stronger connection between CDs and MOS generated by in situ growth of CDs in the matrix. Su et al. reported that the rGO-CDs composite prepared using one-pot green synthetic method has intensively advantages of sensitivity compared to samples fabricated by mechanical mixing methods [26]. Cheng et al. successfully prepared In2O3/CDs nanospheres through one-step hydrothermal method, and the as-fabricated sensor exhibits the superior sensing properties in detecting NO2 [27], including high response (130 to 500 ppb NO2) and low detection limit (9 ppb). Currently, there are fewer reports on the gas sensing properties of SnO2 composites prepared by this method, and the reaction mechanism is not yet clear, especially in the TEA assay.

In this work, SnO2/CDs nanocomposite and SnO2 were produced through simple one-pot hydrothermal method. Compared to pristine SnO2, the SnO2/CDs sensor exhibits improved gas sensitivity and shortened response time, excellent gas selectivity and reduced detection limits. The comprehensive factors including structure, morphology and composition were explored to discuss the reason for improved sensor performance.

Stannic chloride pentahydrate (SnCl4·5H2O) was obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (C6H8O7, CA, ≥ 99.5%), urea (CH4N2O, 99.0%), sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O, SA) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent co., Ltd., China. All the chemicals used in the experiment were of analytical grade without further purification. Deionized water and absolute ethanol (C2H6O) were used throughout the experiments.

SnO2 were prepared through hydrothermal treatment of Stannic chloride pentahydrate and sodium citrate, as described previously [28]. Typically, 1.12 g of stannic chloride pentahydrate, 0.25 g of SA and 0.5 g of sodium hydroxide were dissolved in 80 mL deionized water. After continuous stirring at room temperature for ten minutes, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 12 h. Finally, the reactor was cooled to room temperature naturally. SnO2 nanoparticles were collected after cleaning, drying and calculating.

SnO2/CDs hybrid nanospheres were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal process. Firstly, 0.8139 g of urea was dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water, and stannic chloride pentahydrate (0.3506 g) and CA (0.5701 g) were added to another 20 mL of deionized water to obtain a mixture solution. Secondly, the urea solution was slowly added to the later mixture solution, and stirred at room temperature for 30 min. Thirdly, the obtained new mixture solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave to react at different temperature (180, 200 and 220 °C) for 12 h to clarify the temperature effect. SnO2/CDs hybrid nanoparticles were formed after cleaning, drying and calcinating.

SnO2 nanocomposites doped with different contents of CDs were prepared by changing the molar ratio of tin tetrachloride to citric acid (1:1, 1:1.5, and 1:2). In this process, the molar ratio of citric acid to urea remains unchanged to promote the formation of CDs.

For comparison, SnO2/CDs hybrids were prepared using a two-step method. Typically, the CDs were firstly synthesized through the hydrothermal process using citric acid and urea [29–31]. Then stannic chloride pentahydrate aqueous solution uniformly mixed with a given mass of CDs (0.075, 0.15 and 0.30 mg, respectively) for hydrothermal treatment to form SnO2/CDs hybrid with different doping amounts. All products were post-treated with the same conditions.

The crystallinity of pure SnO2 and SnO2/CDs hybrids was detected by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Smart Lab (9 kW), Rigaku). The microstructure was investigated using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Merlin Compact, Carl Zeiss NTS GmbH) equipped with an energy-dispersive spectrometer (EDS, OXFOFD), transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The combined states of elements were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using an ESCALAB-250Xi spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (4000–500 cm-1) were acquired on STA8000-Frontier (PerkinElmer) spectrometers to investigate the structural details of samples. UV–Vis absorption spectra were examined using a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using a F97 (Prism Technology) fluorescence spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and measurement of gas sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Gas sensing measurements were carried out on a gas sensing system of WS-60 A (Winson Co. Ltd., China). A certain amount of the sample was mixed with ethanol and then ground until a uniform paste was obtained. The paste was slowly coated onto the Ag/Pd interdigital electrode, which has good stability and conductivity like recent advanced electrodes [32–34], and then calcined at 200 °C for 12 h to form a continuous thin coating layer. The sensor performance was measured using a computerized control system including a heatable vacuum chamber and a data acquisition system. The response of a gas sensor is represented by S = R a/R g, where R a and R g are the resistance of the sensor in air and target gas, respectively. Response time is the time required for the response change to reach a 90% stabilized value after injecting gas. The recovery time is the time required for the sensor to recover to 90% of its original response in the air after removing the target gas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characteristics of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1a illustrates the XRD patterns of pristine SnO2 and SnO2/CDs hybrid. For two samples, the characteristic peaks at 26.8°，34.96°，38.17°，52.17°，54.91°and 65.76° are consistent with the (110), (101), (200), (211), (002) and (301) planes of tetragonal rutile SnO2 structure (JCPDS Card No. 41–1445). Compared to SnO2, SnO2/CDs hybrid exhibits a significant expansion at about 26.8° with the half height width of the peak changing from 1.5 to 2.1°, which is representative of carbon material [27]. In order to clarify the introduction of CDs, the fluorescence characteristics were measured. The fluorescence spectra of CDs，SnO2 and SnO2/CDs hybrid under the excitation of 340 nm are shown in Fig. 1b. The pure CDs display the bright blue emission at 441 nm, while SnO2 has no fluorescence emission. Importantly, the fluorescence is also observed for SnO2/CDs hybrid, which is attributed to the introduction of CDs in the hybrid. Compared with pure CDs, the SnO2/CDs hybrid exhibits strong blue fluorescence at 428 nm, which is a slight blue shift due to the interaction between SnO2 and CDs [25]. The absorption spectra and the band energy of CDs and SnO2/CDs hybrid are shown in Fig, S1. There has the absorption peak centered at 330 nm for CDs, which is blue shift to 300 nm for SnO2/CDs, presenting the presence of n-π* transition of CO bond. These functional groups can generate defects and provide abundant reaction sites on the surface of the hybrids [35].
340 nm excitation.

Figs. 2(a) and (b) show the low-magnification SEM images of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs. The SnO2 sample is spherical in shape with a diameter of about 400 nm and a rough morphology. SnO2/CDs hybrid exhibits a more uniform spheroid with a particle size of approximately 200–300 nm. Fig. 2c and d are the EDS spectra of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs hybrid, where the peaks of Sn, O and C are clearly observed in both samples. The C peak in SnO2 sample is attributed to the application of sodium citrate as complexant, while that in hybrid comes from critic acid as carbon source for obtaining CDs. According to the EDS analysis shown in Table 1 , it can be concluded that the relative atomic percentage of C increases significantly from 36.7% for SnO2 to 46.6% for SnO2/CDs, implying the formation of carbon material in the composite material.

The TEM and HRTEM images of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs hybrids are shown in Fig. 3 . As can be seen in Fig. 3a, numerous highly well-regulated spheres with uniform size are readily identified, and the average size of SnO2 is 5 nm. An inter planar spacing of 0.33 nm is observed, corresponding to the (110) lattice planes of SnO2 (see Fig. 3b). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) of SnO2 further proves its tetragonal rutile phase structure, as shown in Fig. 3c. For SnO2/CDs hybrids, the connection between SnO2 and CDs can be observed from Fig. 3d, and the HRTEM image (Fig. 3e) displays two fringes spacing of 0.32 and 0.24 nm. The former corresponds to the (110) lattice fringes of SnO2, and the later may derive from the (1120) facet of graphene, indicating that the crystalline cores of CDs consist of graphitic sp2 carbon atoms [36]. The SnO2/CDs hybrid material exhibits diffraction rings similar to that of SnO2 sample. A batch of primary nanoparticles integrate together to form a large spheroid with rough surfaces, as observed in the SEM images. To further investigate the inner architectures of SnO2/CDs hybrid, the nitrogen adsorption - desorption isotherm and corresponding BJH pore size distribution plots are analyzed and shown in Fig. S3. The N2 isotherm of hybrid sample displays a type-IV isotherm with a large type H3 hysteresis loop [37], and there is no any limiting adsorption at high P/P0. The BET surface area is as high as 67.1 m2/g. The pore size distribution presents that SnO2/CDs hybrids contain a large amount of mesopores with an average size of about 7.5 nm [38]. The large specific surface area provides more reaction sites, which is favorable for improving the performance in the sensor.

FTIR analysis was carried out to further characterize the structural details of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs hybrids. For the both samples, the similar infrared absorption peaks are observed, as shown in Fig. 4 . The strong and wide band at 3263 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of absorption peak of OH. The strong band at 2931 cm−1 is associated with the asymmetric stretching vibration of CH, while that at 1342 cm−1 is a the symmetric stretching vibration of CC. Meanwhile, the SnO2 sample exhibits stronger stretching vibration of Sn-OH at 1560 cm−1 compared to the hybrid material. Noticeable, the SnO2/CDs hybrids present more obvious absorption bands at 1643 cm−1 and 1070 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of CO and C-O-C deriving from CDs, respectively. It is evident that the specific surface functional groups of carbon dots are effectively retained in the SnO2/CDs hybrids.

Fig. 5 illustrates the XPS spectral scans of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs. As can be seen, both samples consist of Sn, O and C elements without any other impurity. The high-resolution Sn3d spectra are displayed in Fig. 5(c, d). The spin orbit splitting of Sn3d3/2 and Sn3d5/2 components in SnO2 sample exists at around 486.75 eV and 495.1 eV. As a comparison, the position of the Sn3d peak in SnO2/CDs slightly shifts to the higher binding energy, implying the presence of interaction between CDs and SnO2. The high-resolution C1s spectrum splits three peaks at 284.8, 286.1 and 289.3 eV, corresponding to C-C/C=C bonds, CO bonds, and O-C=O bonds, respectively (see Fig.S4) [20,26]. Comparing to pure SnO2，the SnO2/CDs composite displays the higher content of CC bond, which might be due to the presence of sp2 C in the CDs. Fig. 5e depicts the high resolution O1s spectrum of SnO2, which could be fitted into three peaks with binding energy at 530.5, 531.1 and 533.8 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen (OL), defect oxygen (OD) and surface adsorption oxygen (OS) respectively. The relative percentages of OL, OD, and OS are calculated to be about 46.3, 26.7, and 27%, respectively. SnO2/CDs possess the similar O1s spectrum, just as shown in Fig. 5f, where, the corresponding relative percentages are approximately 41, 39 and 19%. The OD content in SnO2/CDs is increased by 12.8%. The presence of more surface defect oxygen implies the increase in active sites, thereby improving the capability to react with test gas molecules [27]. This is in consistent with the results of the FT-IR analyses mentioned above, further confirming the successful preparation of SnO2/CDs architecture with efficient bonding connection.

The gas sensors based on pristine SnO2 and SnO2/CDs hybrid were fabricated and the responses to TEA were measured. The gas sensitivity of sensors to 100 ppm TEA is shown in Fig. 6a. Both of them show an “increase-maximum-decrease” tendency with the operating temperatures increasing from 200 to 350 °C. The sensor based on SnO2/CDs provides the highest response to TEA at an operating temperature of 275 °C, which is below the SnO2 sensor temperature (300 °C). The synthesis temperature affects the particle size and crystallinity of CDs [ 39], therefore, the gas sensing response of the SnO2/CDs hybrids prepared under different conditions (180, 200 and 220 °C) is also investigated. At an operating temperature of 275 °C, as the preparation temperature increases, the maximum sensitive responses of the corresponding sensors are 47, 55 and 52, respectively. The results indicate that the optimal synthesis temperature is 200 °C. For the pristine SnO2, the highest response value to 100 ppm TEA is 40 at 300 °C, which is lower than that of SnO2/CDs hybrids. In order to clarify the effect of CDs on gas-sensitive properties, composite materials with different CDs doping were synthesized at this optimum synthesis temperature. Among these samples, the sensor based on SnO2/CDs with the ratio of 1:1.5 presents the greatest response, as shown in Fig. S5. CDs contain the active functional groups such as CO and -OH, whose incorporation into SnO2 improves the selective adsorption and reactivity towards TEA. As a result, the gas-sensitive response is enhanced. However, excessive introduction of critic acid could generate more CDs, and result in the great increasing of static resistance. This effect on resistance exceeds the change in resistance induced by the reaction with TEA, thus leading to a reduced gas response [23].

We compared the gas sensing performance of SnO2/CDs hybrids prepared by two synthesis strategies, as shown in Fig.S6. Comparing with the pristine SnO2 sensitive material, the SnO2/CDs hybrid obtained through the two-step method shows a higher sensitivity to TEA when an appropriate amount of CDs (0.15 mg) is doped, implying that the introduction of active site of CDs influences the gas response [25]. More importantly, the SnO2/CDs hybrid prepared by the simple one-pot hydrothermal method has the best sensing properties. In this synthetic strategy, the reducing process is accompanied by in-situ generation of CDs and SnO2, which is beneficial to strengthen the connection of them and form a more uniform heterojunction [26].

The dynamic response of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs sensors to TEA are illustrated in Fig. 6b. The gas sensors exhibit an excellent response behavior in a wide concentration range of 10 to 1000 ppm. Obviously, the sensing response of SnO2/CDs is superior to that of SnO2 at the same concentration of TEA. For SnO2/CDs sensor, the corresponding responses increase from 3.0 to 15 with the TEA concentration changing from 10 to 50 ppm. Interestingly, the sensing response has two linearly proportional to TEA concentration, with the first stage in the region of below 100 ppm and the other one between 100 ppm and 1000 ppm (Fig. 6c). At lower concentration of TEA, the response slopes in the linear regime for the SnO2 and SnO2/CDs sensor are 0.27 ppm−1 and 0.55 ppm−1, respectively. Within the higher concentration range, the response slopes decrease to 0.108 and 0.188 ppm−1, respectively. When the gas concentration is relatively low, the sensitive material provides enough active sites to adsorb gas, ensuring that TEA molecules can be sufficiently and quickly absorbed by the active substance and then participate in the surface reactions. The active sites are rapidly consumed with the further increase of TEA concentration and the adsorption and desorption processes of TEA molecules on the surface of materials tend to balance, resulting in a slow increase of the sensor response value [40]. At low concentrations, there is an excellent linear relationship between the response and the target gas concentration, which offers the possibility for quantitative detection of TEA gas. The theoretical limit of detection (LOD) can be estimated by using standard method (3σ/s) [41]. σ and S represent the standard deviation (SD) and the slope of sensing curve, respectively. The LOD are calculated to be 90 ppb for SnO2/CDs and 630 ppb for SnO2, which are considerably below the human toxicity level of TEA exposure (threshold limit value of 1 ppm).

A comprehensive comparison of sensing performance is conducted [17,18,42–44] to evaluate the TEA sensor based on SnO2 related sensing materials prepared by the chemical synthesis methods, as listed in Table 2 . SnO2 composite sensors consisting of two metal oxides have relatively high detection limits for TEA, e.g., 500 ppb for SnO2/SiO2 nanocomposites. In the present work, when CDs with abundant functional groups are grown in situ to form SnO2/CDs hetero-composites, their sensor show relatively superior detection limits (90 ppb). The hydrothermal method is suitable for the preparation of gas sensitive materials doped with carbon-based nanomaterials to optimize the sensitive response.

Selectivity is considered as a significant criterion for gas sensors. Fig. 6d compares the response of the SnO2 and SnO2/CDs sensor towards a series of gas with same concentration, including ethanol (C2H6O), acetone (C3H6O), methanol (CH4O), ammonia (NH3), methylbenzene (C7H8) and TEA. The response value of SnO2/CDs-based sensor is <25 to other interfering gases, while the value for TEA gas, is as high as 55, meaning excellent selectivity.

The response/recovery characteristic towards 100 ppm TEA was investigated. It is noteworthy that the response time of the SnO2-based on sensor is 53 s, while that of SnO2/CDs sensor is shortened to 31 s, as shown in Fig. 7a. The shortened response time may be attributed to the high activity of the CDs surface that favors the adsorption reaction of TEA gas and oxygen molecules [27]. The response of SnO2/CDs based sensor to 100 ppm TEA was measured over five reversible cycles to evaluate its repeatability, and the results are shown in Fig. 7b. It can be seen that the response to TEA does not fluctuate within five cycles, exhibiting good repeatability. The stability of gas sensitivity in the composite sensor was examined, as described in Fig. 7c. As time progressed to 10 days, the sensitivity towards 100 ppm TEA diminished from 55 to 46, representing a 16% reduction. Subsequently, there was minimal variation over time, with the sensitivity consistently maintaining at 42 even after 30 days, indicating excellent stability of the sensor. Fig. 7d displays the gas-sensitive response of the composite sensor to TEA at relative humidity (RH). As the increasing of RH = 33%, the response exhibits the clearly decreasing to 18. Comparing to the environment with an RH = 11%, the gas sensitivity is reduced by 90% when the RH reaches 95%. The main reasons are proposed as the adsorption of water molecules on the surface of SnO2/CDs, and the adsorbed water molecules not only hamper the formation of negative oxygen ions, but also restrict the movement of TEA molecules on the sensor surface. As a result, the sensor's response to TEA decreases significantly. Furthermore, TEA molecules prefer to react with OH− of water molecules than adsorbed oxygen. In general, all of these factors result in a decreased sensing response [45,46].

SnO2 is well-known n-type semiconductor metal oxides, and the surface resistance control is a generally accepted gas sensing mechanism [47–49]. When the sensor is exposed to air, oxygen molecules adsorb on the surface of SnO2/CDs to form oxygen ions (O2 −, O−, O2−) by capturing free electrons from the conduction band of sensing materials due to the strong electron negativity of the oxygen atom [50]. As a result, an electronic depletion layer (EDL) is formed on the surface of the sensing materials, causing a decrease in carrier concentration. Normally, the adsorbed oxygen ions are influenced by environmental temperature, which mainly exist in the form of O− at 100–300 °C as Eqs. (1) and (2).

O 2
ads

O 2

O −
ads

When the sensor is placed in TEA, the gas molecules react with the chemisorbed oxygen ions, thus sending the trapped electrons back to the sensitive materials. The electron depletion layers and the height of potential barrier are reduced, and the conductivity of material is enhanced as shown in Eqs. (3) [25].
(3)
C 2
H 5
3
N +
O −
→
H 2
O +
CO 2
+
NO 2
+
e −

Compared to pristine SnO2, the improved sensing properties of SnO2/CDs hybrid are mainly attributed to the synergistic effect of SnO2 and CDs. The work functions of SnO2 and CDs are 4.9 eV and 5.37 eV [27,51–53], and the heterojunctions are constructed at their interface in the composite. Fig. 8 shows the energy band structure diagram of pristine SnO2 and SnO2/ CDs hetero-contact. For the hybrid, electrons are transferred from SnO2 to CDs until the Femi energy levels are equal. The charge transfer leads to band bending around surface and the formation of an electron depletion layer [54]. The characteristic energy band structure of the composite allows for a wider depletion layer than pure SnO2, which means that the corresponding gas sensor has a higher resistance (R a) when exposed to air. When the sensor is exposed to reducing TEA gas, the adsorbed oxygen ions react with the triethanolamine and electrons are released back to the surface of the sensitive material, leading to a decrease of the resistance. The resistance curve of SnO2 and SnO2/CDs are shown in Fig.S7. Unlike pristine SnO2, the released electrons might combine with charge in CDs, and further increasing the elections concentration [52]. As a result, the diffusion of carriers is weakened and the potential barrier height of depletion layers is also decreased, which further decreases the resistance of SnO2/CDs sensor. On the other hand, the CDs-excited surface functional groups and defect features may contribute to enhancement of reactive sites for gas adsorption and result in an uplifted energy band bending, favoring the electrons to passing through the depletion region [55]. In short, compared with pristine SnO2 sensor, synergistic effect of SnO2 and CDs greatly increases the resistance in air and further decrease the resistance in TEA gas. Therefore, based on the definition of response (S = R a/R g), the response to TEA is greatly improved due to the variation of resistance.

In summary, we have demonstrated a highly sensitive and selective SnO2/CDs heterostructure TEA Gas sensor. SnO2/CDs nanocomposite were synthesized by friendly and low-cost one-pot hydrothermal method, and CDs were introduced in-situ and formed PN heterojunction with a space charge layer at the interface between SnO2 and CDs. Compared with the pristine spheroid SnO2, the SnO2/CDs nanocomposite shows much more excellent sensing performance in response, selectivity and detection limit to TEA. The sensitivity of SnO2/CDs sensors to 100 ppm TEA at 275 °C is 55, which is about 1.8 times that of the pristine SnO2 sensor. In addition, the SnO2/CDs sensor also exhibits the limit detection as low as 90 ppb. The enhanced TEA sensing performance of SnO2/CDs sensor is mainly attributed to the formation of the heterojunction and increased active sites, which can promote the reaction between TEA and adsorbed oxygen ions. This work provides an effective way for design and fabrication of the gas sensors with high sensing performances.

Yuehong Yin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yukun Liu: Data curation, Investigation, Resources, Visualization. Youchao Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Yujie Cheng: Formal analysis, Visualization. Yongchao Li: Data curation. Guohua Cao: Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Haitao Zong: Writing – review & editing. Ming Li: Writing – review & editing. Lingling Yan: Investigation. Baoqing Zhang: Project administration, Supervision, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in the manuscript entitled “SnO2/carbon dots nanocomposite synthesized by one-pot solvothermal technique for high-sensitivity triethylamine gas sensors”.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research is supported by Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province, China (Grant No. 232300421203), the Key Science and Technology Program of Henan Province, China (Grant No. 202102310228) and Basic scientific research business cost project of Henan Polytechnic University, China (Grant no. NSFRF210328).
